focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

At 3:07 AM, while Carmen was back in the medication room recounting the shift's controlled substance log, the call bell from Room 116A went off. She was trying to make the numbers come out the way Agnes had taught her to make them come out — carefully, with specific purpose, each discrepancy accounted for in the parallel ledger she now kept in her head.

She was in the hallway before the second chime. Lakeview at night was its own creature — half-lit corridors, the ventilation cycling down to save on electricity, every sound traveling farther than it should. She heard her sneakers on the linoleum, the distant hum of the ice machine, and Room 110's soft continuous moan, which had become as ambient to her as highway noise on her drive home.

At Room 116A, she pushed the door open.

Earl was standing between the two beds in his undershirt and pajama pants, barefoot on the cold linoleum, and he had one of the facility's thin white towels pressed against the side of Burt's head. His other hand was braced on the nightstand for balance. His breathing was heavy, wet, and the effort of getting out of bed and crossing three feet of floor had cost him something she could hear in the rattle of each inhale.

Burt was on the ground. He'd gone down between the bed and the nightstand — the gap was barely eighteen inches — and he was wedged there on his side, one arm pinned under him, the other reaching toward nothing. His legs were tangled in the sheet that had pulled off the bed with him. The bedside rail was down. It shouldn't have been down. She'd put it up at the start of her shift — she was almost sure she'd put it up — but the rails at Lakeview were old and the latches were loose and Burt moved in his sleep sometimes, reaching for things that weren't there anymore....
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ber through the windshield, flattening everything into its own photograph. A plastic bag skidded across the asphalt and caught on a shopping cart someone had left near the dumpster. The courtyard trees were starting to turn — just barely, the first suggestions of yellow at the edges of leaves that had been green all summer.

Carmen sat with her hands on the steering wheel, her key in the ignition, her foot on nothing, and looked at the building she'd spent the day protecting.

Beige stucco. Small windows with institutional blinds, half of them crooked. A sign that read LAKEVIEW GARDENS REHABILITATION AND NURSING CENTER in letters that had faded from green to a color that was no longer any color in particular. No lake. No garden. Three scrubby trees in a paved courtyard and a view of Route 1 if you stood on your toes.

Inside that building, Harry Zielinski was either staring at the parking lot or staring at 1979. Agnes Chen was in her room with Doris breathing in the bed by the window, the click, the pause, the drain. Lola Fernandez had her rosary. Earl Washington had his magazine. Burt Kovacs was still studying his hands.

Carmen Reyes, LPN, license number 26NR03891, had lied to the state to protect a controlled-substance diversion operation running out of Lakeview Gardens.

What stayed in her body was the voice she'd used for it. It was the same calm voice she used for med pass, the same pauses, the same dry annoyance at other people's paperwork. Her mother was calling about Reggie. Carmen let the phone ring out. She sat there fifteen minutes while the amber light went gray and the vinyl under her palms cooled. When the screen lit again, she put the key the rest of the way in, started the car, backed out of her space, and drove to her mother's house to pick up her son.
